summary: Four-perspective research sweep for boat winterization + storage service design
logs: research complete: taxonomy=ok rates=ok platform=ok risk=ok

scenarios.name: Mobile round-trip (owner's A): winterize at customer property, boat stays home, spring recommission on site
steps: FALL - Customer: makes boat accessible (on lift pulled onshore, on its trailer in driveway, or blocked in yard) and confirms last-use date

FALL - Vendor: on-site engine winterization (fuel stabilizer, fog cylinders, drain/antifreeze block and manifolds for sterndrive/inboard, gear lube for outboards), drain freshwater/livewell/ballast systems
FALL - Vendor: optional on-site add-ons (battery pull or disconnect, shrink wrap or cover fit, rodent packs)
WINTER - Customer: boat sits at customer property; customer bears weather, snow-load, and rodent risk
SPRING - Vendor: returns after ice-out to de-winterize (reinstall/charge batteries, unfog, impeller/plug check, flush, remove wrap) and test-run on muffs or at the dock
SPRING - Either: launch - vendor floats it off trailer/lift or customer splashes it; requires the lift/pier to already be set (hard dependency on pier and lift services)
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Customer stages the boat and owns winter custody; vendor does all mechanical work both legs; launch is either party. No transport at all.
vendorTypesOffering: Pole-barn shop that also runs mobile
scenarios.prevalence: Common - the default for pontoons/outboards at seasonal lake homes with room to store; the bread-and-butter mobile transaction on small Indiana lakes
constraints: Boat must be out of the water or on a lift for most sterndrive/inboard work; outboard pontoons can be done on the lift
Fall crunch: must complete before first hard freeze (block-crack risk); platform pull-deadline (hard freeze minus 8 days) is the real backstop
Spring leg cannot precede ice-out and usually cannot precede lift/pier set - sequencing dependency the router must enforce
Shrink wrap on site needs a dry boat and workable temps; wrapping in late Nov cold is miserable and slower
Wake/ski boats with ballast bags and heaters take 2-3x the mobile labor of a pontoon - price and slot accordingly

scenarios.name: Full-service valet (owner's B): vendor hauls out, winterizes at shop, stores at vendor facility, recommissions and relaunches in spring
steps: FALL - Vendor: retrieves boat - floats it onto a hydraulic/pontoon trailer at a ramp, or lifts off the customer's dock/lift
FALL - Vendor: transports to shop; winterizes indoors (full engine + systems)
FALL - Vendor: shrink wraps (if outdoor yard) or blocks/racks indoors; logs storage location
WINTER - Vendor: custody, insurance, and optional battery tending; prime window to sell winter repair/upgrade work
SPRING - Vendor: de-winterizes, details if sold, launches at ramp or sets boat back on the customer's lift
SPRING - Customer: confirms delivery; lift/pier must be set before redelivery
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Vendor does everything both directions; customer only books and pays. Vendor holds custody all winter.
vendorTypesOffering: Pier/lift contractor partnered with a mechanic (split-vendor)
scenarios.prevalence: Common but capacity-capped - the premium product; indoor slots frequently sell out by Labor Day and vendors with hydraulic trailers are scarce
constraints: Requires hydraulic trailer or dedicated pontoon trailer - the single scarcest asset in the market; most boats on these lakes (lift-kept pontoons) have NO trailer of their own
Ramp access: small glacial lakes have few, shallow public ramps; deep-draft inboards can be hard to retrieve late season when levels drop
Fall route density matters - vendors batch pickups by lake; platform should cluster jobs
Commercial tow/garagekeeper insurance required for custody and transport - a real vendor-qualification gate
Spring redelivery is gated by ice-out AND lift set AND ramp usability; everyone wants back in by Memorial Day
Storage tier (heated indoor / cold indoor / outdoor wrapped) changes price and what add-ons are needed

scenarios.name: Customer-transport store (owner's C): customer tows both ways, shop winterizes + stores + de-winterizes
steps: FALL - Customer: tows boat on own trailer to shop by appointment
FALL - Vendor: winterizes at shop; wraps if outdoor storage
WINTER - Vendor: stores boat (usually on customer's trailer, sometimes blocked with trailer stored separately)
SPRING - Vendor: de-winterizes on schedule or on customer's call; notifies when ready
SPRING - Customer: picks up, tows to lake, launches self
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Customer does all transport and launch; vendor does all mechanical work and holds winter custody.
vendorTypesOffering: Storage-only operator partnered with a mobile tech (split-vendor)
scenarios.prevalence: Very common for trailer-owning customers - fishing boats, ski boats, PWCs; cheapest full-package path since no vendor transport
constraints: Only works if the customer actually owns a roadworthy trailer - excludes most lift-kept pontoons
Boat typically occupies its trailer all winter; some shops charge extra to store boat and trailer separately
Drop-off and pickup appointment slotting creates fall/spring counter congestion - platform scheduling helps here
Spring notification-to-pickup lag ties up yard space; vendors want pickup deadlines

scenarios.name: Mobile winterize-only: fall service with NO storage arrangement and NO spring leg booked
steps: FALL - Customer: stages boat (lift/trailer/yard)
FALL - Vendor: engine + systems winterization on site; optional wrap/battery add-ons
WINTER/SPRING - Customer: everything else - storage, de-winterize, launch are the customer's problem (or a later separate booking)
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Vendor does one visit; customer owns everything else. Fall and spring are decoupled transactions.
scenarios.prevalence: Very common - likely the single highest-volume transaction type in the market; many owners DIY the spring because it is easier than winterizing
constraints: Must be sold as a standalone leg - platforms that force a bundled spring leg lose this volume
Spring leg should be offered as an upsell at winterize time and again at ice-out
Same freeze-deadline crunch as scenario A

scenarios.name: Shop winterize, home storage: customer tows in, vendor winterizes, customer tows home to own pole barn/garage
steps: FALL - Customer: tows boat to shop
FALL - Vendor: winterizes (same-day or 1-3 day turnaround); optional wrap if customer stores outdoors
FALL - Customer: tows boat home and stores it in own outbuilding
SPRING - Either: customer DIYs recommission and launch, or books a mobile spring visit / tows back to shop
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Customer transports both ways and stores; vendor does fall mechanical only.
scenarios.prevalence: Very common in rural Indiana - pole barns and machine sheds are everywhere; owners want pro winterization but free storage
constraints: Fast turnaround expected - customer does not want two round trips a week apart
No vendor winter custody, so no storage revenue; shops still like it for throughput
Spring is naturally decoupled - platform should prompt a spring-mobile upsell

scenarios.name: Storage-only: boat arrives already winterized, vendor just stores it
steps: FALL - Customer (or another vendor): winterizes the boat elsewhere
FALL - Either: customer tows to storage, or storage vendor picks up (transport add-on)
WINTER - Vendor: indoor (heated or cold) or outdoor storage; no mechanical work; optional battery tending
SPRING - Either: customer retrieves, or vendor delivers (transport add-on)
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Vendor provides space and custody only; all mechanical work is someone else's. Transport either party per leg.
vendorTypesOffering: General contractor/farmer with barn or lot
scenarios.prevalence: Common - a large silent market; ag buildings and contractor barns absorb huge boat volume every winter at $30-60/ft dry rates
constraints: Vendor must disclaim mechanical liability - boat must arrive winterized in writing (waiver) or storage vendor requires proof/partner winterization
Access policy matters: many barn operators are dead-storage (no mid-winter access, boats packed nose-to-tail)
This is the archetype that lets non-marine vendors (contractors with land) join the platform
Outdoor storage effectively requires shrink wrap or a mooring cover

scenarios.name: Shrink-wrap-only: wrap service with no mechanical winterization
steps: FALL - Customer or another vendor: engine already winterized (or outboard owner skipping it)
FALL - Vendor: shrink wraps boat on site or at shop (frame, film, vents, optional zipper door)
SPRING - Either: vendor returns to remove and dispose of wrap, or customer cuts it off
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Vendor wraps only; customer handles all mechanical and storage decisions.
vendorTypesOffering: Pier/lift contractor crews (trainable low-skill-ceiling add-on trade)
scenarios.prevalence: Moderately common as a standalone; near-universal as an add-on - some seasonal crews do nothing but wrap for 8 weeks
constraints: Boat must be dry and reasonably clean before wrapping
Late-fall temperature and wind make on-site wrapping slow and risky - film and propane heat gun in the wind
Wrap disposal in spring is a real cost line; recycling access is spotty in Indiana
Pricing is per foot plus tower/arch surcharges - fits the platform's per_foot model

scenarios.name: Spring-only recommission / splash service: no fall relationship
steps: WINTER - Customer: boat was winterized by customer, a different vendor, or came with a winter purchase
SPRING - Vendor: de-winterizes (batteries, unfog, impeller, flush, systems check), removes wrap, safety check
SPRING - Either: vendor launches/delivers to lift, or customer launches; a bare 'splash-only' variant is just transport + launch with no mechanical work
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Vendor does spring mechanical and optionally launch; customer did or bought the fall work elsewhere.
scenarios.prevalence: Moderately common - DIY winterizers who want a pro spring check, and every boat bought over the winter needs one
constraints: Extreme compression: nearly all demand lands in the 3-4 weeks after ice-out
If the vendor did not winterize it, they will not warranty freeze damage - expect a documented pre-check
Splash-only needs the same trailer/ramp assets as scenario B
Lift/pier set is again the upstream dependency

scenarios.name: Hybrid: vendor hauls in fall, customer retrieves in spring
steps: FALL - Vendor: picks boat up from dock/lift/ramp, winterizes at shop, stores
SPRING - Vendor: de-winterizes and notifies
SPRING - Customer: tows home or launches self (e.g., customer has a trailer but no time in the fall crunch, or wants the boat at a different lake in spring)
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Vendor does fall transport + all mechanical + storage; customer does spring transport/launch.
scenarios.prevalence: Niche but real - asymmetric transport shows up whenever the customer's fall and spring situations differ
constraints: Pricing must split transport per leg, not bundle round-trip
Custody handoff and 'ready for pickup' notification need explicit states in the platform

scenarios.name: Hybrid: customer tows in fall, vendor delivers and launches in spring
steps: FALL - Customer: tows to shop (has trailer and time in fall)
FALL/WINTER - Vendor: winterizes and stores
SPRING - Vendor: de-winterizes, then delivers to the lake and launches / sets on lift (customer is absent, wants the boat waiting at the dock Memorial Day weekend)
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Customer does fall transport; vendor does everything else including spring delivery.
scenarios.prevalence: Niche-to-moderate - very attractive to absentee seasonal owners, which is exactly LakeLife's customer base
constraints: Spring delivery needs hauling equipment even though fall didn't - vendor capability check is per-leg
Delivery-to-lift requires lift set first; gate codes / dock access on delivery day
The 'boat is in the water when you arrive' promise is a flagship concierge SKU

scenarios.name: Transport-only hauling: standalone move with no mechanical work
steps: ANY - Vendor: moves the boat between any two points - lake to customer's own barn, lake to third-party storage, shop to lake, lake to lake
ANY - Customer: books the leg; someone else (or no one) does the mechanical work
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Vendor is purely a hauler; customer or other vendors handle everything else.
vendorTypesOffering: Pier/lift contractor with hydraulic or pontoon trailer
scenarios.prevalence: Niche as a consumer-facing SKU but constant in reality - it is the glue leg inside most split-vendor arrangements
constraints: Hydraulic trailer or correct-bunk pontoon trailer required; width permits for 10 ft+ pontoons on road
Commercial auto + on-hook insurance
Priced per move + distance; platform should expose it so other scenarios can be composed from it

scenarios.name: Split-vendor bundle (platform-orchestrated): mobile tech winterizes at dock + hauler transports + barn owner stores
steps: FALL - Vendor 1 (mobile tech): winterizes at the customer's dock or driveway
FALL - Vendor 2 (hauler): picks up and delivers to storage
WINTER - Vendor 3 (barn/lot owner): stores; no mechanical liability because Vendor 1 documented winterization (photos)
SPRING - Vendor 2: returns boat to lake; Vendor 1: recommissions on the lift; platform sequences all of it
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Three specialized vendors each do one leg; the PLATFORM is the general contractor presenting one price and one schedule to the customer.
vendorTypesOffering: Composed from: Mobile marine tech + hauler + land/barn owner - none of whom could sell scenario B alone
scenarios.prevalence: Rare as an advertised retail product, but common informally (shops subcontract hauling and rent barn space constantly) - this is LakeLife's structural advantage: manufacture full-service capacity out of partial vendors
constraints: Requires per-leg job states, per-leg payouts, and custody handoff records (photo-at-handoff)
One customer price, three vendor costs - margin math per leg
Failure of one leg (hauler no-show) strands the others - needs the claim-board/waitlist machinery
Liability boundaries must be explicit: mechanical vs transport vs storage damage

scenarios.name: PWC / jet ski winterize + rack storage (multi-unit)
steps: FALL - Either: vendor collects PWCs (several fit one trailer) or customer drops off
FALL - Vendor: winterizes (exhaust antifreeze, fog, stabilize, battery out on tender)
WINTER - Vendor: racks them - PWCs stack densely, high revenue per sq ft; or mobile variant: winterize on the customer's PWC lift and leave in the garage
SPRING - Either: vendor recommissions and redelivers to lift, or customer picks up
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Same leg permutations as boats but batched - households often have 2-3 units; vendor typically transports because PWC lifts outnumber PWC trailers.
scenarios.prevalence: Common - nearly every lake house on these lakes has at least one PWC; per-unit pricing with a multi-unit discount matches reality
constraints: Cheap per unit, so route density and batching decide profitability
Supercharged models (some Sea-Doo/Yamaha) need more than the basic service - flag by model
Freeze damage risk is identical to boats - same deadline pressure

scenarios.name: Lift and canopy offsite winter storage (extension of lift pull/set)
steps: FALL - Vendor: pulls the boat lift and canopy frame from the water (existing lift-pull service), removes canopy fabric
FALL - Vendor: hauls lift to vendor yard instead of leaving it on the customer's shoreline; stores fabric indoors (mildew/rodent)
WINTER - Vendor: outdoor lift storage in rows at yard
SPRING - Vendor: returns and sets lift before the boat comes back - it is the first domino of the spring sequence
scenarios.whoDoesWhat: Vendor does everything; customer just lacks shoreline/yard space to keep the lift.
scenarios.prevalence: Niche-to-moderate - most lifts overwinter on the owner's shore, but small-lot and association properties pay for offsite; canopy-fabric indoor storage is a popular sub-service
constraints: Barge or shore-crane access for pull/set (already solved by existing lift service)
Spring ordering: lift set must beat boat redelivery - platform already models this dependency
Canopy fabric stored damp will mildew - must dry before storage

addons.name: Shrink wrap (per foot + tower/arch surcharge)
addons.appliesTo: Any fall scenario; effectively mandatory for outdoor storage
addons.notes: Includes frame, vents, film; zipper access door is a common sub-add-on. Boat must be dry/clean; cold and wind slow on-site wrapping. Fits platform per_foot pricing.

addons.name: Shrink wrap removal + disposal (spring)
addons.notes: Separate line item vendors always charge; wrap recycling access in Indiana is spotty so disposal is a real cost.

addons.name: Battery removal, winter tending (trickle charge), spring reinstall
addons.notes: Per battery. Wake boats carry 2-4. Vendor-stored variant only when vendor has a facility; mobile variant is disconnect-in-place or store-in-customer-garage on customer's charger.

addons.name: Engine oil + filter change (fall, pre-storage)
addons.notes: Best practice is fall (acids in used oil sit all winter). Highest-attach add-on at winterize time.

addons.name: Lower unit / gear lube change + water-intrusion check
addons.notes: Milky lube in fall reveals a seal problem BEFORE it freezes and cracks the case - genuinely protective, easy to sell.

addons.name: Fuel stabilizer + fuel system treatment
addons.notes: Usually bundled into the base winterize; listed so vendors who itemize can.

addons.name: Ballast system winterization (wake/ski boats)
addons.notes: Bags, pumps, lines, heaters must be drained/antifreezed. Materially more labor - should be a surcharge or separate SKU, not silently absorbed.

addons.name: Freshwater system / head / livewell winterization
addons.appliesTo: Cruisers, pontoons with sinks/changing rooms, fishing boats
addons.notes: Non-toxic antifreeze through plumbing; often overlooked by DIYers and a cause of spring claims.

addons.name: De-winterize / spring recommission package
addons.notes: Batteries in, unfog, flush, systems check, test run. The core spring SKU (existing $430 flat maps here).

addons.name: Spring tune-up (plugs, impeller, filters)
addons.notes: Impeller every 2-3 seasons on sterndrives/inboards; natural spring attach.

addons.name: Detail / wax
addons.notes: Spring detail on stored boats is easy winter/early-spring shop revenue; can be its own vendor archetype (detailer).

addons.name: Hull / pontoon tube cleaning (scum line acid wash, tube brightening)
addons.notes: Glacial-lake hard water leaves scale on pontoon tubes; best done right at haul-out while growth is wet.

addons.name: Trailer service (bearings, lights, tires, brakes)
addons.appliesTo: Any scenario where a trailer exists
addons.notes: Fall or spring; prevents the spring-tow breakdown. Natural add-on for scenarios C and shop-winterize.

addons.name: Winter trailer storage (boat stored off-trailer)
addons.notes: Some shops block the boat and rack trailers separately for density; small fee.

addons.name: Summer trailer storage (trailer parked at vendor while boat is in the water)
addons.notes: The inverse-season service the owner likely missed - lake lots have nowhere to put a trailer all summer; cheap recurring revenue for any vendor with a lot.

addons.name: Heated indoor storage upgrade (vs cold indoor vs outdoor)
addons.notes: Three-tier storage pricing is the industry norm (roughly outdoor < cold indoor < heated); heated skips some winterization steps but reputable shops winterize anyway.

addons.name: Mid-winter checks / battery tending subscription
addons.appliesTo: Vendor-stored boats; premium mobile variant for home-stored boats
addons.notes: Monthly wrap/strap/battery check with photo report - fits LakeLife's photo-proof DNA perfectly.

addons.name: Rodent protection (deterrent packs, covers sealed, bait boxes outside boat)
addons.appliesTo: All storage, especially barns and home yards
addons.notes: Mice are the number-one winter damage source in barn storage; cheap add-on that prevents expensive upholstery/wiring claims.

addons.name: Winter repair / upgrade work (engine work, upholstery, electronics, gelcoat)
addons.notes: The biggest missed revenue stream: shops sell off-peak labor into stored boats all winter. Platform should support estimate-approve-complete flow on stored jobs.

addons.name: Prop inspection / reconditioning
addons.appliesTo: Fall (send out over winter) or spring
addons.notes: Winter is when props go to the prop shop; classic stored-boat add-on.

addons.name: Delivery + launch / splash fee
addons.appliesTo: Any spring leg with vendor transport
addons.notes: Priced per move; separate from mechanical work so hybrids and split-vendor bundles can price per leg.

addons.name: Haul-out fee (float-on or lift-off retrieval)
addons.appliesTo: Any fall leg with vendor transport
addons.notes: Counterpart to launch fee; surcharge when no trailer exists and vendor must bring a pontoon/hydraulic trailer.

vendorTypes.type: Mobile marine tech (truck + tools, no facility)
capabilities: On-site winterize/de-winterize at dock, lift, or driveway
Shrink wrap on site
Battery service (disconnect/in-place or customer-garage storage)
Oil/gear lube changes, small repairs on site
PWC mobile winterize
Winterize-only, spring-only, and both legs of the mobile round-trip
cannotDo: Winter storage of any kind
Hauling/transport (no hydraulic trailer, no custody insurance typically)
Major repairs needing a shop or hoist
Anything requiring the boat to leave the property

vendorTypes.type: Marina with service yard (on-lake or nearby)
capabilities: Everything: haul-out, shop winterization, indoor/outdoor storage, shrink wrap, spring recommission, launch/redelivery
Hydraulic and pontoon trailers, sometimes forklift
Winter repair/upgrade work on stored boats
Trailer summer storage
Warranty-adjacent service if also a dealer
cannotDo: Scale beyond physical yard/building capacity - indoor sells out by Labor Day
Cheap mobile-only visits economically (shop overhead makes travel work expensive)
Fast response during crunch weeks - service queue is the bottleneck

vendorTypes.type: Independent shop with pole barn (rural Indiana staple)
capabilities: Shop winterization and de-winterization
Cold indoor (sometimes heated) storage
Shrink wrap at shop
Winter repair work
Hauling IF they own a pontoon/hydraulic trailer - roughly half do
Customer-transport scenarios always; full-service only with hauling gear
cannotDo: On-water/at-dock work unless they also run mobile
Heated storage (most are cold barns)
Warranty work on major brands
Full-service B without a hauling partner - prime split-vendor candidate

vendorTypes.type: General contractor / farmer with land and buildings
capabilities: Storage-only: outdoor lot or dead-storage barn
Lift and canopy offsite storage
Transport sometimes (trucks, trailers, telehandlers exist on farms)
Summer trailer parking
cannotDo: ANY mechanical winterization or recommissioning - must require proof of winterization or a bundled tech (liability)
Shrink wrap (usually)
Mid-winter boat access (dead storage, packed tight)
Insurance-grade custody unless they add a garagekeeper policy - platform should verify

vendorTypes.type: Dealer service center (brand-affiliated, may be off-lake)
capabilities: Shop winterize/store/recommission, warranty and recall work
Pickup/delivery programs with proper equipment and insurance
Winter refit and rigging work
Handles the complex boats (supercharged PWCs, wake boat ballast/surf systems) other vendors decline
cannotDo: Cheap or fast - premium labor rates and long queues
Mobile dock-side work usually
Compete on storage price with barn operators

vendorTypes.type: Pier/lift contractor (already on LakeLife) expanding into boat logistics
capabilities: Haul-out and redelivery (barge, shore equipment, trailers already owned)
Lift/canopy pull, offsite storage, spring set
Splash-only and transport-only legs
Shrink wrap as a trainable add-on trade
Perfect sequencing partner: they already control the lift-set date the boat legs depend on
cannotDo: Engine/mechanical winterization (not marine techs)
Warranty or repair work
Storage unless they have yard space - many do

missedScenarios: Winterize-only with no spring leg and no storage - probably the highest-volume transaction in the market; fall and spring must be decoupled, sellable separately, with spring offered as an upsell at winterize time and again at ice-out
Shop-winterize + home-storage: customer tows in, vendor winterizes, customer tows home to their own pole barn - huge in rural Indiana, zero storage revenue but high throughput
Storage-only for already-winterized boats - opens the platform to non-marine vendors (contractors/farmers with barns and lots) and needs a proof-of-winterization/waiver mechanic
Shrink-wrap-only as a standalone SKU (and wrap REMOVAL as a separate spring SKU with disposal fee)
Spring-only recommission and splash-only launch - DIY winterizers, and every boat purchased over the winter
Asymmetric transport hybrids: vendor hauls fall / customer retrieves spring, and customer tows fall / vendor delivers-and-launches spring - transport must be priced per leg, not round-trip
Transport-only hauling as a composable leg (lake to own barn, lake to third-party storage, lake to lake)

Split-vendor orchestration: mobile tech + hauler + barn owner composed by the platform into one full-service product none of them could sell alone - LakeLife's structural moat, requires per-leg states, per-leg payouts, and photo custody handoffs
PWC multi-unit batching with per-unit pricing and household discounts (2-3 units per home is normal on these lakes)
Lift and canopy offsite winter storage plus canopy-fabric indoor storage - natural extension of the existing lift pull/set service
Summer trailer storage (May-Oct) while the boat is in the water - the inverse-season service; lake lots have no trailer parking
Mid-winter care subscription: battery tending, strap/wrap checks with photo reports - fits the platform's photo-proof model, works for vendor-stored AND home-stored boats

Winter repair/upgrade work sold into stored boats (engine, upholstery, electronics, prop reconditioning) - the industry's biggest off-season revenue stream; needs an estimate-approve-complete flow on jobs in storage status
Storage tiering as a pricing dimension (outdoor wrapped / cold indoor / heated indoor) rather than one flat storage product
New-purchase spring commissioning and delivery for boats bought over the winter (no fall relationship exists)

In-water overwintering with de-icers/bubblers - list only to explicitly EXCLUDE it: essentially nonexistent and inadvisable on small hard-freeze glacial lakes where piers and lifts come out; de-icers around permanent piers are a different (rare) product

Sequencing dependencies as first-class platform logic: ice-out gates spring work, lift/pier set must precede boat redelivery, winterize must precede hauling to a no-mechanical storage vendor, and the pull-deadline (hard freeze minus 8 days) backstops every fall leg

rates.service: Winterization labor — outboard engine (fog, fuel stabilize, drain; gearcase check)
rates.notes: Larsen Marine $109 labor-only single / $199 twin (materials extra); Midwest Winterizing $180 all-in; Just Add Water (Indianapolis) $250 (0-149hp) / $300 (150hp+); Timber Marine $175-340 by HP/tier; Loon Marine 4-stroke $180-460 by HP. Lower-unit gear oil change is a common add-on: $88-106 (Larsen). All-in typical for the 90-300hp pontoons/fishing boats on small Indiana lakes: $180-300.
rates.source: Larsen Marine (Waukegan IL) 2025 price guide; Midwest Winterizing (Twin Cities MN); Just Add Water Boats (Indianapolis IN); Timber Marine Services (Hayward WI); Loon Marine Service

rates.notes: Basic (fog/stabilize/drain/antifreeze) $200-390: Larsen $244 labor-only single / $434 twin; Midwest Winterizing $315; Just Add Water $325 basic, $375 Bravo/Volvo; Timber $200-390. 'Complete' incl. engine oil+filter runs $400-520 (Loon 4cyl-big block V8). National guide range $300-600.
rates.source: Larsen Marine 2025; Midwest Winterizing; Just Add Water Boats (Indianapolis); Timber Marine Services; Loon Marine Service; premiumboatcare.com

rates.service: Winterization labor — true inboard / V-drive (ski & wake boats)
rates.notes: Larsen $252 labor-only single gas (oil+filter +$174, transmission oil +$95); Twin Lakes Marine (WI) inboard from $396 + parts; Loon complete $400-520 (+$60 V-drive fluid); Midwest Winterizing $600 all-in for inboard rear-motor. National guides put inboards at $400-800; ballast/heater systems on wake boats push toward the top.
rates.source: Larsen Marine 2025 Inboard guide; Twin Lakes Marine (WI); Loon Marine Service; Midwest Winterizing; premiumboatcare.com

rates.notes: Industry prices pontoons at the outboard-engine rate (by HP) — no pontoon premium on the engine work itself. The premiums show up elsewhere: shrink wrap billed at a higher per-foot tier ($19/ft vs $16/ft at Midwest Winterizing), tri-toon transport +$40-75/trip (Timber), and $50-85 trailer rental per move if the owner has no trailer (Pointe Marine $50/haul; Timber $85 rental).
rates.source: Midwest Winterizing; Just Add Water Boats; Timber Marine Services; Pointe Marine Association (MI)

rates.notes: $100-200 is the common shop range (2-stroke low end, 4-stroke high end); Midwest Winterizing $250/engine with 10% multi-engine discount; Timber $135-275; Loon jet drive $160 (2-stroke) / $300 (4-stroke); Just Add Water jet boats $275. Winterize+indoor-storage combos for PWC run $350-700 flat.
rates.source: Outdoor Troop / Bayside Jet Drive survey; Midwest Winterizing; Timber Marine Services; Loon Marine Service; Just Add Water Boats

rates.notes: Midwest Winterizing $16/ft (≤23'), $19/ft pontoons & 24'+; Minnesota Shrinkwrapping (mobile, MN/WI) $17-19/ft by LOA; Timber $22-23/ft; Foster Bros (Delano MN) $25/ft; Just Add Water (Indianapolis) $30/ft. Larsen bills per sq ft ($2.80-3.40/sqft = roughly $24-29/LOA-ft on an 8.5' beam) plus $75 removal/disposal fee. PWC wrap: ~$200 flat. Wrap is single-season; includes zipper door + mildew protection at full-service yards.
rates.source: Midwest Winterizing; Minnesota Shrinkwrapping LLC; Timber Marine Services; Foster Bros Marine; Just Add Water Boats; Larsen Marine 2025

rates.notes: Midwest Winterizing $12/ft bare, $22/ft with shrink wrap; Timber $25/ft misc / $30/ft with wrap; Just Add Water outdoor $35/ft; Manitowoc Marina (WI) flat tiers $970 (≤24') to $2,075 (≤44') ≈ $40-52/ft but includes travelift haul, bottom wash, and spring launch. PWC outdoor: $250-400 flat (Timber). Bare-lot small-lake pricing clusters $12-25/ft; full-service (haul+wash+launch included) $35-50/ft.
rates.source: Midwest Winterizing; Timber Marine Services; Just Add Water Boats; Manitowoc Marina

rates.notes: Great Lakes states outdoor-uncovered self-storage/lot rate; covered outdoor $60-130/mo. Monthly billing is a self-storage-facility norm — marinas overwhelmingly sell the whole winter season, not months.

rates.service: Winter storage — indoor cold (unheated building, seasonal)
rates.notes: Timber indoor $38/ft; Just Add Water (Indianapolis) flat $895-1,525 for 16'-30' (≈$50-56/ft), Oct 15-Apr 15; Torresen Marine (Muskegon MI) $5.15/sqft ≈ $44/LOA-ft on 8.5' beam. PWC indoor $325 single / $550 double (Timber). Monthly equivalent at Great Lakes self-storage: $100-250/mo.
rates.source: Timber Marine Services; Just Add Water Boats; Torresen Marine (Muskegon MI); FindBoatStorage

rates.unit: per sq ft (LOA x beam), per season
rates.notes: Torresen (Muskegon) $7.30/sqft; West Michigan Dock & Market $8.00/sqft incl pullout, bottom wash, blocking, spring launch; Manitowoc Marina $10.25/sqft; Pier 33 (St. Joseph MI) $11.15 early / $15.00 late; Southport Marina (Kenosha WI) $12.50/sqft. On an 8.5' beam that's ≈ $62-128/LOA-ft — a 2-3x premium over cold indoor. Monthly self-storage equivalent $150-400.
rates.source: Torresen Marine; West Michigan Dock & Market Corp; Manitowoc Marina; Pier 33; Southport Marina

rates.service: Haul-out / local boat transport (vendor trailers boat, <20 miles, one-way)
rates.notes: Timber $150 (1-10 mi) / $225 (11-20 mi), tri-toon $225/$265; Just Add Water local lakes $250-300 with customer present, $300-350 without; Foster Bros $350 for BOTH legs (fall pickup + spring drop-off) within 25 mi ≈ $175/leg; Pointe Marine (MI) free within 20 mi when bundled with storage, then $3.60/mile. Add $50-85 trailer rental per move if the boat has no trailer.
rates.source: Timber Marine Services; Just Add Water Boats; Foster Bros Marine; Pointe Marine Association

rates.notes: Timber spring prep $99 (storage customers) / $155 (non-customers); one MI inland marina $115; simple recommission (battery, fluids check, run-up) $100-200; national full-commissioning range $200-800 for boats with water systems/AC/gensets — inland pontoons and runabouts sit $100-300. Often discounted or bundled free for winter-storage customers.
rates.source: Timber Marine Services; Club Bennington forum (MI marina); TPG Marinas / seaboundmagazine survey

rates.notes: Dry-stack/forklift launch $25-75 per splash; Pepin Marina (WI) spring launch $5.30/ft trailered, $7.74/ft non-trailered, $3.50/ft travelift drive-over (≈$106-155 for a 20' boat); public ramp fees $5-20/day (WI DNR caps un-approved fees at $13/day). Full-service seasonal storage almost always includes one spring launch in the storage price.
rates.source: Pepin Marina (WI); Wisconsin DNR fee schedule; storage.com dry-stack survey; Manitowoc Marina (included example)

rates.service: Bundled package — winterize + shrink wrap + storage + spring prep (20-24 ft boat)
rates.notes: Lake Ogemaw Marina (MI) advertises complete fall packages 'starting at $335' (small boats: pull, wrap, winterize, store, spring prep, dock). Mid-market build-up: Midwest Winterizing 22' I/O = $315 winterize + $22/ft outdoor-with-wrap ≈ $800. Indoor high end: Just Add Water 22' ≈ $1,100 indoor storage + $325-375 winterize + transport ≈ $1,700-1,800. National shorthand: 'about $500 winterize incl wrap + spring dewinterize' at full-service marinas. Bundles typically discount 10-20% vs a-la-carte and fold the spring launch in free.
rates.source: Lake Ogemaw Marina (West Branch MI); Midwest Winterizing (computed); Just Add Water Boats (computed); boatsetter.com

rates.service: Add-on — engine oil & filter change (with winterization)
rates.notes: Larsen: outboard 4-stroke $94, gas I/O $174 (+$12 disposal); Midwest Winterizing $105 (+$15 synthetic); Just Add Water $175-300 by HP, Mercury I/O $250 / Volvo $275 (+$50 big block). Most shops require it be combined with winterization in fall.
rates.source: Larsen Marine 2025; Midwest Winterizing; Just Add Water Boats

rates.service: Add-on — winterize fresh water system (cottage-style boats)
rates.notes: Larsen $73 (≤26'), $166 (27-40'); Just Add Water $275. Head winterize $45-150; pump-out $82. Rare on small inland lakes but applies to larger cruisers.
rates.source: Larsen Marine 2025; Just Add Water Boats

rates.service: Late pickup / overstay after storage season ends
rates.notes: Just Add Water (Indianapolis): $10/DAY after May 1. Midwest Winterizing: $30/MONTH after season end (May 18). Both patterns exist; per-diem is the sharper and increasingly common one. Late PAYMENT is separate: 1.75%/mo or $50/mo minimum in standard marina storage contracts.
rates.source: Just Add Water Boats; Midwest Winterizing; River Bend Marina / Westin's Marine Center storage contracts

billingNorms: Winter storage is sold as a SEASON, not by the month: one flat price covers the whole winter regardless of exact in/out dates. Per-foot-of-LOA-per-season is the dominant unit at inland Midwest yards ($12-38/ft); large Lake Michigan yards bill per square foot (LOA x beam, $2.80-15/sqft); flat length-tier pricing (e.g. 16'-30' brackets, Manitowoc's $970-$2,590 tiers) and flat PWC pricing are the other common shapes. True monthly billing ($40-400/mo) is a self-storage-facility pattern, not a marina pattern.

Full-service storage prices commonly INCLUDE haul-out, pressure wash, blocking, and one spring launch (Manitowoc Marina, West Michigan Dock & Market, Pointe Marine); winterization, oil changes, and shrink wrap are always billed separately on top. Cheaper bare-lot storage ($12-25/ft) includes nothing.

Deposit/balance pattern A (reserve-then-settle): signed contract + non-refundable deposit reserves the space; ~50% deposit with balance due by ~March 31 / before spring launch. Pattern B (bill-at-haul-out): full storage invoice issued when the boat goes into storage, due ~November 1. Universal rule either way: the boat does not launch in spring until the account is paid in full.

Overstay per-diems start ~May 1: $10/day (Just Add Water, Indianapolis) or $30/month (Midwest Winterizing, after May 18). Late payment on invoices runs 1.75%/month or $50/month minimum in standard storage contracts.

Quote style splits by shop type: full-service yards (Larsen) quote LABOR ONLY with oil, filters, antifreeze, and materials additional plus environmental/disposal fees ($12/engine oil disposal, $75 shrink wrap disposal); mobile services and small-lake shops quote ALL-IN flat prices. All-in flat pricing is what homeowners on small Indiana lakes expect.

Engine-count and HP drive winterization price, not boat length: twin-engine adds ~80% of the single rate (Larsen $109->$199 outboard, $244->$434 I/O); outboard tiers break around 90-150hp; multi-PWC discounts ~10%. Boat LENGTH drives wrap, storage, and launch pricing.

Shrink wrap is single-season and per-foot, with pontoons billed one tier higher than runabouts ($19 vs $16/ft at Midwest Winterizing); removal/disposal in spring is either bundled or a separate $75 fee.

Transport is billed per one-way move in distance bands (1-10 mi / 11-20 mi / 20+ mi at $3.50-3.60 per extra mile), with a tri-toon surcharge (+$40-75) and $50-85 trailer rental per move when the owner has no trailer; storage customers often get free transport within ~20 miles as a bundle perk.

Spring prep/commissioning is routinely discounted for storage customers ($99 vs $155 at Timber) or folded in free as bundle bait; spring launch is included in full-service storage but $25-75 (or $3.50-7.74/ft) a la carte.

Proof of insurance is required for storage (Just Add Water; standard in storage contracts), and winter-storage packages are commonly marketed with fall pickup + spring delivery included within a radius (Burt Lake Marina: free within 20 miles).
rates.seasonDefinitions: The Midwest winter storage 'season' is a single ~7-9 month block sold at one price: typical contract windows are Sept 1 - May 31 (Timber Marine, Hayward WI, 9-month contract), Sept 15 - June 15 (Manitowoc Marina WI), mid-Sept - mid-May (Midwest Winterizing MN), Oct 1 - Apr 30 (Westin's Marine contract), and Oct 15 - Apr 15 (Just Add Water, Indianapolis indoor). In practice boats come out late September through early November (ahead of hard freeze) and go back late April through May (after ice-out); the contract window is deliberately wider than actual use and costs the same whether the boat arrives in September or November. Overstay per-diem/monthly fees begin around May 1 - May 18. Summer storage is a separate, cheaper product (roughly Apr 15 - Oct 15; High & Dry in Angola IN charges $600 flat outdoor for that window; Timber sells summer months at $100-125/mo). For LakeLife's Steuben/LaGrange County lakes this maps cleanly onto the existing pull-deadline (est. hard freeze minus 8 days, i.e. storage intake peaks Oct-Nov) and ice-out (spring splash-back and launch demand peaks mid-Apr to mid-May).

options.name: Option 1 — Separate service row per package (SKU-per-scenario)
options.description: Each delivery scenario becomes its own row in `services`: 'Winterize at your dock (mobile)', 'Haul-out + shop winterize + storage', 'You-tow shop winterize + storage', 'Winterize only', 'Storage only', plus add-on rows (shrink wrap, battery care) as separately bookable services. Storage-bearing SKUs use the new `seasonal_plus_perdiem` pricing model; the fall and spring visits are booked as two SKU jobs (e.g. 'Haul + winterize + store' in fall, 'De-winterize + relaunch' in spring) linked only informally.
pros: Dispatch matching needs ZERO code change: `vendors.service_types[]` + `vendor_rates` per service already gate by SKU — the mobile tech simply never lists the haul SKU, the barn owner never lists the mobile SKU
Rate cards stay exactly as built: one `vendor_rates` row per (vendor, SKU), priced through the existing `priceService`
One-price rule holds trivially: every SKU resolves to one number via the existing engine; nothing new for the vendor-price firewall
Photo gate works unchanged and can differ per SKU (`services.min_photos` — the haul SKU can demand at-dock + on-trailer + in-rack shots)
Fastest to ship for the Oct–Nov 2026 beta; the seed file just grows
cons: SKU explosion: 3 scenarios × (with/without storage) × add-ons multiplies rows; the customer menu clutters and ops must keep N overlapping price rows coherent

The fall→spring lifecycle has no first-class link: spring de-winterize is a separate booking the customer must remember to make, per-diem overage has no anchor row to compute days from, and vendor stickiness (the boat is physically at THAT barn) is unenforced
Add-ons as separate services create separate jobs that could dispatch to a DIFFERENT crew than the one holding the boat — nonsense for shrink wrap on a stored boat
Capability drift is honor-system: nothing structural stops a vendor without a hydraulic trailer from listing the haul SKU

options.name: Option 2 — One service + selectable priced components in JSONB (option groups)
options.description: Keep a single 'Boat winterize & storage' service row; extend `band_pricing` params with customer-selectable option groups (scenario: mobile/haul/you-tow; storage: yes/no; add-ons: shrink wrap per-foot, batteries flat). The chosen options are stored on the job (`jobs.options jsonb`); price = sum of selected component prices. Vendor capability comes from new `vendors.capabilities text[]` flags ('haul','indoor_storage','shrink_wrap') and per-option rates inside `vendor_rates.band_pricing`.
pros: One clean menu tile with a wizard — matches the roadmap's 'wizard adds: store-with-us vs self-store, splash-into-which-lake' UX intent
No SKU explosion; ops edits one row's params to reprice everything
Pricing rules still live in the DB (components are data in band_pricing), honoring rule 8
cons: Breaks the engine's core contract: `priceService(rule, profile) → number` must become option-aware, and `vendor_rates` no longer mirrors `services` cleanly — the crew rate card becomes a per-option matrix every vendor must fully fill in

Dispatch matching gets genuinely harder: eligibility now depends on the job's selected options, not the service name — `isEligible`/`canClaim` need a capabilities gate PLUS a per-option rate-completeness check (a crew missing a shrink-wrap rate silently can't take a wrapped job), and the margin floor becomes ambiguous (per component or per package?)
Photo gate weakens: `min_photos` is per service, but a mobile 3-photo winterize and a haul+rack job need different counts — you'd need per-option photo minimums, new server enforcement code
Still one job for a two-visit season: split fall/spring billing and per-diem require job splitting anyway, so the 'one service' simplicity is illusory

options.name: Option 3 — Package = bundle of component services, one job per visit (RECOMMENDED)
options.description: Model the primitives as component rows in `services` (winterize_mobile per_foot, winterize_shop per_foot, haul_out flat-or-per_foot, de_winterize, relaunch/return_visit, storage_season using new model `seasonal_plus_perdiem`, shrink_wrap per_foot, battery_care flat), flagged with a `kind` column (standalone/component/addon). New `service_packages` + `package_components` tables define the customer-facing bundles (Scenario A/B/C, winterize-only, storage-only) with each component tagged fall-phase or spring-phase. Booking creates a `job_groups` envelope (property, package, ONE boat) plus one job per VISIT; `job_items` line-items tie each visit job to its components with per-component customer_price and vendor_cost. Customer sees one all-in package price = sum of component menu prices, each computed by the existing `priceService`.
pros: Dispatch stays per-job and structurally capability-gated: a crew is eligible for a visit only if `service_types` covers ALL of that visit's components with positive `vendor_rates` — components ARE the capability flags, so the mobile tech physically cannot receive a haul job

Rate cards keep the exact current shape: one `vendor_rates` row per (vendor, component service), same table, same pricing engine, same RLS — vendors still never see menu prices (job_items vendor_cost is ops-only like jobs.vendor_cost)

One-price rule holds at the right altitude: the package quotes one number at booking (stored on job_groups.total_quoted); per-visit allocation is internal and invisible to the customer until the two receipts

Photo gate needs ZERO new enforcement code: each visit job carries its own min_photos (sum of its components') and rides the existing complete-action → settleJob path; fall intake photos and spring relaunch photos are independently gated
`seasonal_plus_perdiem` lands cleanly as the pricing model of exactly one component, one new switch case in priceService, and split fall/spring billing falls out of one-invoice-per-job for free
Vendor stickiness is expressible: the spring leg of a stored boat pre-pins to the storing vendor via the job_group
cons: Most schema work: 4 new tables (service_packages, package_components, job_groups, job_items) plus a storage_stays ledger, and the booking wizard must become multi-visit aware
Spring jobs are born dateless ('reserve now, pick date when ops opens the window') — needs the two-phase booking rails the roadmap already calls for, but they don't exist yet
Ops repricing is indirect: changing a component's rate changes every package containing it (arguably correct, but a behavior change to explain)

decideDispatch needs a real change: crewRate becomes the SUM of the visit's component rates and the margin floor is checked against the visit's summed menu price, plus a new seasonal (not daily) storage-capacity gate

platform.recommendation: Option 3 — package as a bundle of component services with one job per visit. Rationale: it is the only option where all four hard rules stay enforced by EXISTING machinery instead of new parallel code. (1) Vendor price-blindness: vendor_rates keeps its per-service shape and RLS; job_items inherits the jobs.vendor_cost ops-only pattern — no new firewall surface. (2) Pricing-in-DB: components and packages are rows; seasonal_plus_perdiem is one new enum value + one priceService case; ops edits rates in data exactly as today. (3) Photo gate: the current complete-action already counts job_photos against the job's service minimum per job — one job per visit means fall intake and spring splash are each independently photo-gated and independently settled with no changes to settleJob's idempotent one-invoice/one-payout-per-job design. (4) Dispatch: capability gating collapses into the mechanism that already exists (service_types membership + positive rate), rather than a new capabilities vocabulary that can drift from rates (Option 2's biggest trap). Option 1 is the honest fallback if the Oct beta timeline slips — it ships in days — but it hard-codes the package combinatorics into the menu, leaves the fall→spring lifecycle unlinked (per-diem has no anchor), and lets under-equipped vendors self-select into SKUs they can't perform. Migration path is friendly: retire the current 'Boat storage & winterize' per_foot row, seed the ~8 component rows and 5 packages, and Option 1-style single-visit packages (winterize-only) are just one-component bundles, so the simple cases cost nothing extra.
schemaDeltas: ALTER TYPE pricing_model ADD VALUE 'seasonal_plus_perdiem' (0001 defines pricing_model as a Postgres enum; note ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE cannot run inside the same transaction that uses it — separate migration) and add the matching case to src/lib/pricing.ts. Rule params in band_pricing jsonb: { seasonal_base, seasonal_per_foot, included_days, perdiem_rate, perdiem_per_foot } — fall leg prices seasonal_base + seasonal_per_foot × boat feet; spring leg prices max(0, days_stored − included_days) × (perdiem_rate + perdiem_per_foot × feet)

services: ADD COLUMN kind text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'standalone' CHECK (kind IN ('standalone','component','addon')) — components/addons are priceable but not directly bookable tiles; existing rows stay 'standalone'

NEW TABLE service_packages (id uuid PK, name text, description text, active boolean, sort int) — the customer-facing bundles: mobile-winterize (Scenario A), full-service haul+store (B), you-tow+store (C), winterize-only, storage-only

NEW TABLE package_components (package_id FK, service_id FK → services, phase text CHECK (phase IN ('fall','spring')), required boolean, default_on boolean, PRIMARY KEY (package_id, service_id, phase)) — e.g. Scenario B fall = haul_out + winterize_shop + storage_season(fall leg); spring = de_winterize + relaunch + storage_season(spring/per-diem leg); shrink_wrap and battery_care attach as required=false addons

NEW TABLE job_groups (id uuid PK, property_id FK, package_id FK, boat_id FK → boats, status text, total_quoted numeric, storing_vendor uuid FK → vendors NULL, created_at) — the season envelope; boat_id fixes the fleet-summing bug: package pricing must use THIS boat's length_ft, not boatFeet() across all boats

jobs: ADD COLUMN group_id uuid FK → job_groups, ADD COLUMN phase text ('fall'|'spring'), ADD COLUMN price_finalized boolean DEFAULT true — spring per-diem jobs are created with price_finalized=false and customer_price recomputed at completion time from actual days stored

NEW TABLE job_items (id uuid PK, job_id FK, service_id FK, customer_price numeric, vendor_cost numeric) — per-component line items; jobs.customer_price/vendor_cost stay as the sums so settleJob, invoices, payouts, and the vendor_jobs price-free view are untouched; REVOKE client writes and mirror the ops-only visibility of jobs money columns (per the Supabase default-write-grants lesson)

NEW TABLE storage_stays (id uuid PK, job_group_id FK, vendor_id FK, boat_id FK, intake_at timestamptz, out_at timestamptz, included_days int, status text ('reserved','in_storage','released','overstay')) — the per-diem clock and the ops answer to 'whose barn is this boat in'; intake_at stamped by fall-job settle, out_at by spring-job settle

vendors: ADD COLUMN storage_capacity_feet integer DEFAULT 0, ADD COLUMN storage_types text[] DEFAULT '{}' ('indoor','outdoor') — storage is a SEASONAL capacity pool, not the daily_capacity slot count; remaining pool = storage_capacity_feet − sum of active storage_stays boat feet

seed_services.sql: retire/deactivate the current 'Boat storage & winterize' per_foot row; seed component rows (winterize_mobile per_foot, winterize_shop per_foot, haul_out, de_winterize, relaunch, storage_season seasonal_plus_perdiem, shrink_wrap per_foot addon, battery_care flat addon) each with their own min_photos, and the five packages
dispatchChanges: Visit-level capability gate: extend isEligible/the loader in src/app/book/dispatch.ts so a crew qualifies for a visit job only if vendors.service_types contains EVERY component service on that job's job_items AND vendor_rates has a positive rate for each — the component list is the capability flag, so 'mobile tech has no storage' and 'barn owner has no hydraulic trailer' are enforced by construction, not by a checkbox

Rate/margin math: crewRate for a visit = sum of the crew's component rates (each computed by the existing priceService against vendor_rates rows); margin floor (decideDispatch's affordable filter and canClaim's rate_too_high) checks summed crew rate vs the visit's summed menu price — one floor per visit, no per-component ambiguity

Storage capacity gate (new, seasonal not daily): for visits containing storage_season, require vendor storage_capacity_feet minus feet already committed in active storage_stays ≥ this boat's length_ft; this sits beside dailyCapacity (which still governs the day's labor slots for the haul/winterize work)

Job-group vendor stickiness: spring legs of a stored boat (Scenario B/C) do NOT re-enter decideDispatch — they pre-assign to job_groups.storing_vendor (the boat is physically at their barn); Scenario A's spring return visit re-dispatches normally with preferredVendorId = the fall crew for right-of-refusal continuity

Two-phase spring scheduling: spring jobs are created dateless at booking ('reserved'); when ops opens the lake's spring window the customer picks a date (roadmap §4 mechanic) and only then does the job hit routes/capacity — remainingCapacity and the calendar must ignore dateless reserved jobs

Claim board: canClaim gets the same all-components-covered + per-component-rate + storage-capacity checks; storage-bearing jobs should arguably NOT be claim-board cold-start items (a stranger crew claiming custody of a boat is a trust event, not a mow) — gate claimability to non-storage visits unless the owner decides otherwise

Photo gate per visit (no code change, config only): fall haul job min_photos = sum of its components' minimums (at-dock, on-trailer, in-rack), spring job likewise (out-of-rack, at-dock running) — the existing completeJob action and settleJob path enforce it unchanged
platform.billingTiming: Split billing, two invoices per package, riding the existing one-invoice-per-job settleJob flow unchanged. FALL: when the fall visit job passes its photo gate and completes, settleJob charges invoice #1 = fall components (haul + winterize + shrink wrap addon) + the storage seasonal minimum (seasonal_base + seasonal_per_foot × boat feet, covering included_days ≈ 210 days / ~7 months Oct–Apr — owner to confirm the roadmap's '~3 months charged at pull' wording vs the real Indiana season) — this matches the roadmap note 'storage seasonal minimum charged at pull'. Fall settle also stamps storage_stays.intake_at and releases the fall payout (crew's fall component rates). SPRING: the spring job is created price_finalized=false; when the customer schedules splash and the job completes its own photo gate, a finalize step recomputes customer_price = spring components (de-winterize + relaunch) + per-diem overage = max(0, days(intake_at → completed_at) − included_days) × per-diem rate, then settleJob charges invoice #2 and releases the spring payout — 'per-diem overage billed at splash', exactly per the roadmap. OVERSTAY: if the boat is still in the barn N days past included_days with no scheduled splash, the existing nightly cron flips storage_stays to 'overstay' and raises a monthly interim per-diem invoice against the job_group (new small settle path — the one genuinely new billing surface), so a never-collected boat can't accrue silently for a year. Card-on-file autopay, credits-first, and receipt behavior all inherit from the current settleJob. Vendor payout split follows the invoices: fall components + storage seasonal share at fall settle, spring components + per-diem share at spring settle (keeps payout-follows-photo-verified-completion intact for both visits).
openQuestions: Billing split confirmation: the roadmap says seasonal minimum at pull + per-diem at splash — confirm the customer ALSO pays the spring de-winterize/relaunch labor at splash (two-receipt model) rather than everything up front at fall; and confirm the included period (the note says '~3 months' but Indiana storage runs Oct–Apr ≈ 7 months — which number is the minimum?)

Per-diem economics: is the seasonal minimum flat per boat or per foot? Is the per-diem overage flat per day or per foot per day? Who keeps the per-diem — the storing vendor at their rate share, or is it margin-weighted like everything else (30% target)?

Abandonment/overstay policy: at what point does an uncollected boat trigger the interim monthly invoice, and is there a hard cutoff (lien/disposal terms)? The roadmap already flags a 'storage terms' ToS page — legal review needed before beta storage bookings
Spring vendor stickiness for Scenario A (mobile, boat stays home): is the spring return visit required to be the same crew, preferred-same-crew, or any qualified mobile crew?
Storage claim-board policy: may a crew claim a storage-bearing job cold (custody of a customer's boat via first-tap), or are storage packages dispatch/preferred-only?

Insurance: is a standard COI sufficient for months-long custody of a $40k boat (fire/theft in the barn), or does the storage_season capability require a storage-specific endorsement flag on the vendor before the gate opens?
Indoor vs outdoor storage: one component priced by band, or two components — and does the customer choose, or the vendor's storage_types decide?
Shrink wrap lifecycle: does the fall wrap price include spring removal/disposal, or is removal a spring component line?
Early exit: customer wants the boat back in January — is the seasonal minimum non-refundable (recommended, matches 'minimum'), and does an off-season retrieval visit price as a separate flat service?
Multi-boat households: one package per boat (implied by job_groups.boat_id — recommended), and does booking two boats into the same barn earn any bundle discount, or strictly linear pricing?

Scenario C drop-off mechanics: when the customer tows to the shop, does the drop-off consume one of the vendor's daily_capacity slots (an appointment) or is it a free-form window with only the storage-feet gate?

risks.risk: Bailment liability: the storage vendor becomes a legal bailee of a $30k-$150k asset for ~6 months — a fundamentally different legal posture than a 2-hour dockside service call.
risks.whyItMatters: Under Indiana common law, a paid (mutual-benefit) bailment imposes a duty of ordinary care with a burden-shifting presumption: if the boat comes back damaged, the bailee is presumed negligent unless they can prove otherwise. One totaled wake boat can exceed a small crew's net worth, and the claim will splash onto the platform reputationally and possibly legally.
risks.mitigation: A written per-job storage agreement that names the vendor (not LakeLife) as bailee, defines the standard of care, caps liability and disclaims consequential damages, and requires the customer to keep hull insurance in force through the lay-up period. Every custody transfer is documented with timestamped, countersigned condition evidence.
risks.productImplication: New e-sign clickwrap 'Storage Agreement' step in the storage booking flow (versioned, stored per job); a custody_transfers table logging every hand-off (timestamp, actor, GPS, photo set); a booking-time checkbox + policy upload proving the homeowner's hull insurance stays active during lay-up.

risks.risk: A standard COI is worthless for this service: general liability policies exclude damage to property in the vendor's 'care, custody, or control' — which is the entire exposure of storage.
risks.whyItMatters: A vendor who passes today's onboarding insurance check is effectively uninsured for the core risk of storing boats. When an uninsured vendor damages a stored boat, the customer's only deep pocket is the platform.
risks.mitigation: Require garagekeepers legal liability (direct-primary basis preferred) or marina operators / bailee's-customer coverage as a precondition for offering storage, with per-boat and aggregate limits sized to the vendor's declared inventory (e.g., $500k+ aggregate). Re-verify every fall before the crunch, not just once at onboarding.
risks.productImplication: Onboarding gains an insurance-requirements matrix keyed to service type: the storage service is unlockable only with a garagekeepers/bailee COI on file; coverage type, per-boat limit, aggregate limit, and expiry become structured fields; policy lapse auto-suspends the vendor from storage dispatch; ops dashboard shows expiry alerts at 60/30/7 days.

risks.risk: Transport is a separate uninsured exposure: garagekeepers generally does not cover the boat while it is on the trailer on a public road ('on-hook'/in-tow), and the truck's auto policy covers the truck, not the cargo.
risks.whyItMatters: The two road legs are statistically the most dangerous hours of the whole six-month engagement — a dropped or crashed boat in transit is the single most likely total-loss scenario, and it is the gap between two policies.
risks.mitigation: Require on-hook/in-tow cargo coverage with limits >= the maximum declared hull value the vendor will haul. Capture the vendor's max tow length/weight and trailer specs so the router never assigns a boat the vendor cannot legally or insurably move.
risks.productImplication: Boat profile gains declared_hull_value, length, beam, and dry_weight; vendor rate card gains max insured hull value and max tow specs; the router adds an insurable-match hard constraint; transport becomes its own job leg with its own status and photo checkpoints (loaded/strapped at origin, arrival at destination).

risks.risk: Damage discovered at spring splash with no agreed baseline — 'that gouge wasn't there in October' — six months and several custody transfers after intake.
risks.whyItMatters: Without a countersigned condition record, both sides argue from memory, and the bailment presumption means the vendor loses by default — so good vendors will refuse the service, or the platform will eat goodwill refunds on every claim. Condition proof is the load-bearing wall of the entire service.
risks.mitigation: Mandatory structured intake and outtake condition surveys with a fixed shot list (hull sides, transom, gelcoat close-ups, prop/skeg, upholstery, engine bay, hour meter, fuel level), damage annotations, and homeowner countersignature within 48 hours or auto-accept. The identical protocol runs in reverse at splash, with a defined dispute window.
risks.productImplication: The photo gate evolves from 'minimum count' to a typed checklist: a condition_report entity with required shot classes per leg, a customer e-acknowledgment step, and a dispute_window_ends field; the spring splash flow opens by showing both parties the fall intake report side-by-side.

risks.risk: Shrink-wrap failures and winter damage attribution: a torn or badly vented wrap causes mold, water intrusion, or snow-load collapse months after the workmanship error — and the wrapper, the storer, and the weather are three different liability buckets, sometimes two different vendors.
risks.whyItMatters: Wrap-failure damage is latent, expensive, and ambiguous by nature; it is the archetypal 'whose fault is winter?' dispute and will consume ops time out of all proportion to its dollar value unless attribution is pre-built.
risks.mitigation: Photograph the completed wrap (supports, vents, belly bands, strapping) as a required leg completion; run a mid-winter inspection cadence with photo proof, triggered after major snow events for outdoor storage; contract language allocating wrap workmanship to the wrapper and post-wrap monitoring to the storer.
risks.productImplication: Storage splits into indoor-heated / indoor-cold / outdoor-wrapped tiers with different prices and inspection SLAs; auto-generated mid_winter_check micro-jobs attached to each storage job; 'completed wrap' becomes a distinct required photo class on the winterize/wrap leg.

risks.risk: The fall crunch: nearly all haul-out demand compresses into the ~2 weeks before the pull-deadline, and capacity is physically bounded by ramps, trucks, trailers, and daylight.
risks.whyItMatters: A missed haul-out is not a rescheduled mow — it is a boat frozen into the lake or a cracked block, a five-figure loss against a hard external deadline the platform itself computes and advertises (pull deadline = est. hard freeze minus 8 days).
risks.mitigation: Open the fall calendar in mid-summer with early-bird pricing that decays toward the deadline; have each vendor declare daily haul capacity; run a per-lake capacity-vs-booked gauge with waitlist and auto-escalation; set a cutoff date after which new bookings are explicitly 'best effort, no freeze guarantee.'
risks.productImplication: Vendor capacity calendar (slots/day per service); per-lake demand-vs-capacity gauge on the ops dashboard; honest scarcity display in the booking flow; a system-controlled 'guaranteed by pull-deadline' badge that stops being offered the moment remaining capacity math no longer supports it.

risks.risk: The spring crunch at ice-out, compounded by a dependency chain: pier must be in and lift set before the boat can splash, and the ice-out date itself is only known days in advance.
risks.whyItMatters: A boat trucked to a lake with no lift in the water is a stranded asset sitting dockside; promising hard splash dates against an unknowable ice-out date guarantees broken promises in the platform's highest-emotion week of the year.
risks.mitigation: Sequenced multi-service scheduling that auto-orders pier install → lift set → splash at the same property; release splash slots only after ice-out is confirmed per lake; sell customers a priority window and a visible queue position rather than a calendar date.
risks.productImplication: Cross-job dependency fields (blocks / blocked_by) in the jobs model; splash booking captures a priority tier + window instead of a hard date; the existing per-lake ice-out confirmation event flips queued splash jobs from 'waiting' to 'schedulable' and notifies homeowners with their queue position.

risks.risk: Mid-winter customer access: 'I sold the boat,' 'I need my gear out of it' — the customer (or a stranger claiming to be a buyer) wants access to a wrapped, racked boat in January.
risks.whyItMatters: De-racking and re-wrapping costs real vendor labor that nobody priced, and releasing a boat to an unverified 'buyer' is conversion — full-asset-value liability and the nightmare custody scenario for the platform.
risks.mitigation: A published access policy (N included scheduled visits, per-visit fee after, X business days notice); gear retrieval as a bookable micro-job; release of custody only via in-app authorization by the verified account holder naming the recipient, who presents a one-time code.
risks.productImplication: Homeowner access-request flow (request → vendor schedules → fee auto-billed); a release_authorization record (authorizer, named recipient, code, timestamp, recipient ID photo) required before the job can move to released_early; the access policy is a linked page inside the storage agreement.

risks.risk: Non-payment while the vendor holds the boat: Indiana's possessory/mechanic's lien route for stored boats is slow and procedural — certified-mail notice to the owner and any lienholders, statutory waiting periods, then a title/sale process through state channels — and doing it wrong creates liability for the vendor.
risks.whyItMatters: A non-paying boat occupies revenue-producing rack space for months while the 'remedy' crawls; individual crews will not know the statute and will improvise (selling or scrapping a boat without proper process), and their improvisation becomes the platform's scandal.
risks.mitigation: Structure billing so nobody is ever deep in arrears: card on file with pre-authorized installments, dunning and service-pause at first failed charge, and 'paid in full before relaunch' as policy — spring splash is the natural leverage point. The storage agreement grants an express contractual lien alongside the statutory one; the platform supplies a lien-process playbook and template notices rather than leaving vendors to freelance.
risks.productImplication: A storage_billing schedule table with dunning states; delinquency badges with day counters on the ops dashboard; a server-enforced rule that a splash leg cannot be scheduled while a balance is outstanding; a later-phase lien-notice letter generator for vendors.

risks.risk: Abandoned boats: the customer ghosts entirely — no payment, no contact — and a derelict pontoon occupies a rack indefinitely; low-value boats can cost more to dispose of than they are worth.
risks.whyItMatters: Distinct from slow-pay because there is no one to dun; the statutory abandonment/lien-sale process requires documented contact attempts, and without pre-built evidence trails the vendor is stuck warehousing a liability for free.
risks.mitigation: An abandonment clause in the storage agreement with a defined trigger (e.g., 60 days past contracted season end plus 3 documented failed contacts across SMS and email); a deposit or final-installment structure sized so disposal costs are covered; platform-verified contact info (already required by rule 5) as the evidentiary backbone.
risks.productImplication: An abandoned_pending job status with automated, logged contact attempts (each SMS/email recorded as evidence for the statutory process); an ops runbook page for the abandonment procedure; version-tracked abandonment clause in the agreement.

risks.risk: Per-diem overstay disputes: the boat stays past the contracted season end — customer not ready, lift not yet set, or customer unresponsive — and nobody agrees who owes daily storage or from when.
risks.whyItMatters: Spring is precisely when vendors need racks empty for turnover; a surprise per-diem invoice is the classic bill-shock dispute, and it lands on the platform because the platform holds the payment relationship. Critically, the overstay is sometimes the platform's own fault (the lift job it scheduled ran late).
risks.mitigation: Fix the contracted end date and per-diem rate at booking, not in April; automated countdown notices at T-30/14/7/1; a grace period; and a fault-attribution step — per-diem is waived when the delay is caused by the vendor or the platform's own scheduling chain.
risks.productImplication: contracted_end_date and per_diem_rate as booking fields shown on the price screen; an automated notice sequence; overstay charges accrue as itemized line items with a displayed reason; an ops-set delay-fault flag (customer / vendor / weather / platform) that gates whether per-diem actually bills.

risks.risk: Mid-season cancellation: the customer cancels in January and expects prepaid storage back, but the vendor's opportunity cost was front-loaded — that rack was reserved through the fall crunch and cannot be re-sold in midwinter.
risks.whyItMatters: Pure pro-rata refunds are unfair to vendors; 'no refunds' feels predatory to customers; ad-hoc ops decisions create precedent chaos. This needs a published schedule before the first booking, not after the first dispute.
risks.mitigation: A published, tiered refund schedule: full refund minus deposit before haul-out; after rack-in, performed legs (haul-out, transport, wrap) are non-refundable and unused whole months refund at a stated percentage; early release also runs through the release-of-custody protocol.
risks.productImplication: Extend the existing cancellation-policy engine with a duration-service policy type (per-leg performed/not-performed logic plus a monthly proration table); the app shows a computed refund preview before the customer confirms cancellation.

risks.risk: Billing timing breaks the platform's core trust promise: 'never charged until work is complete' has no coherent meaning for a 6-month duration service.
risks.whyItMatters: Charging everything upfront abandons the promise and creates a large refund liability sitting on the platform; charging only at spring completion makes the vendor float six months of cost and concentrates non-payment risk at the exact moment the boat must be released. Trust and cash flow pull in opposite directions unless the promise itself is redefined.
risks.mitigation: Redefine the promise per milestone: storage is explicitly sold as a multi-leg service where each leg bills on its own completion — haul-out/winterize charged when done, storage billed as disclosed fall/spring installments (or monthly) against the card on file, splash leg charged at completion.
risks.productImplication: Jobs model gains legs with independent billing events; the services table gains a duration/installment pricing model (per rule 8, this lives in the DB, not code); the booking screen shows a payment-timeline graphic; the marketing trust-promise copy gets a storage-specific variant: 'you're charged as each milestone completes.'

risks.risk: The photo-gate rule (rule 2) does not map to multi-leg custody: modeled as one job, the gate fires once at spring completion, leaving haul-out, transport, wrap, and rack-in completely unevidenced — exactly where the disputes live.
risks.whyItMatters: The photo gate is the platform's core enforcement mechanism and its main defense in every custody dispute; a six-month evidentiary blind spot between booking and completion neutralizes it precisely for the highest-value service on the menu.
risks.mitigation: Decompose storage into discrete legs — haul-out, transport-in, winterize/wrap, rack-in, mid-winter checks, de-rack, transport-out, splash — each carrying its own required photo classes and its own payout release, server-enforced at the leg level.
risks.productImplication: Schema change: a job_legs table with per-leg minimum photo counts and typed photo classes; the payout ledger releases per leg rather than per job; the server-side photo gate is re-implemented at leg granularity; the homeowner app shows a boat-journey timeline with photos at every hop.

risks.risk: Marketplace liability position: if the terms are silent on who the bailee is, a platform that sets the customer price, brands the service, and holds the money looks like the principal, not a venue — and plaintiffs will sue everyone.
risks.whyItMatters: One serious claim (sunk trailer load, burned facility) could pull LakeLife into a bailment it never priced or insured. The platform's hidden-margin, one-price model actively strengthens the 'LakeLife was my counterparty' argument, so the paper trail has to push the other way explicitly.
risks.mitigation: Terms state explicitly that the vendor is the independent bailee and custodian and LakeLife is a coordination platform; vendor insurance is primary; require LakeLife as additional insured on the vendor's garagekeepers and on-hook policies where obtainable; carry a platform-level contingent/excess liability policy as a backstop.
risks.productImplication: A storage-specific terms addendum with its own versioned acceptance checkbox for both homeowner and vendor; an onboarding upload slot for the additional-insured endorsement; a claims-intake flow (report damage → freeze payouts on affected legs → assemble the condition-report evidence bundle → route to the vendor's insurer).

risks.risk: Seasonal price-gouging optics: scarcity pricing during the fall crunch reads as gouging ('it was $485 in August, why is it $900 in October?') in a small-lake community where everyone compares notes.
risks.whyItMatters: Customers already cannot see vendor cost or margin; unexplained seasonal jumps burn the one-price trust model that justifies that opacity. But refusing to price the crunch wastes the platform's best lever for smoothing demand away from the pull-deadline wall.
risks.mitigation: Frame the curve as an early-bird discount, not a late surcharge: anchor the peak price, publish the full-season price calendar the day booking opens, decay the discount on a disclosed schedule, and cap the peak-to-base spread.
risks.productImplication: The services pricing model gains date-banded pricing rows in the DB (rule 8); the booking UI shows the whole season's price curve upfront ('book by Sept 1: $X'); an ops-configurable cap on the peak/base ratio; this reuses the existing scarcity-surcharge machinery with mandatory disclosure.

risks.risk: Vendor failure mid-winter: the storage vendor goes out of business, loses their lease, or their building burns or collapses under snow load with thirty customer boats inside — a correlated, catastrophic loss.
risks.whyItMatters: Every boat in one facility fails simultaneously, in midwinter, when relocation capacity is near zero — and it is exactly the scenario where customers look straight past the crew to the platform that took their money. The platform must know, at all times, which boats are physically where.
risks.mitigation: Facility vetting at storage onboarding (structure type, snow-load adequacy, fire suppression, security, photos); every stored boat linked to a specific facility address; a contingency playbook where ops can trigger mass relocation using other storage vendors as backstop capacity; contingent platform insurance.
risks.productImplication: A storage_facilities entity (address, type, capacity, vetting checklist, photos) linked to each storage job; a facility-level incident flag that fans out notifications to every affected homeowner at once; facility capacity feeds the same demand-vs-capacity gauge as the fall crunch.

risks.risk: Split winterization: the customer stores with a LakeLife vendor but winterized the engine themselves or elsewhere — the block cracks over winter and the storer is holding the boat when it is discovered.
risks.whyItMatters: Freeze damage is the most expensive common winter loss and is invisible at intake; when winterization and storage are split across parties, each blames the other, and the bailee presumption unfairly pins it on the storer.
risks.mitigation: Bundle aggressively (winterize + store discount, which also feeds the existing $485 winterization service); if unbundled, require proof of professional winterization or a signed waiver excluding freeze damage to unverified engines from storer liability; record engine winterization status on the intake report.
risks.productImplication: The intake condition report gains a winterized_by field (linked LakeLife job / third-party receipt upload / owner-DIY waiver checkbox); the booking flow nudges the bundle; the freeze-damage waiver is a clause in the storage agreement triggered by that field.

risks.risk: Boat spec mis-declaration: the customer under-declares length or weight, so the per-foot price is wrong and the assigned vendor may be unable to legally or insurably tow the boat — discovered as a wasted truck roll in the worst week of the fall crunch.
risks.whyItMatters: Per-foot pricing already depends on declared length, but storage raises the stakes: an overweight/oversize surprise is a road-legality and insurance problem, not just a pricing one, and it burns a crunch-week slot that cannot be resold.
risks.mitigation: Verify length and beam at haul-out as part of intake (tape-measure photo), then run the existing vendor-flag mechanic (rule 6): correction proposed → homeowner approves → profile updates and repricing happen atomically; mark the boat profile verified after first measured intake.
risks.productImplication: Reuse the vendor-flag/approve/reprice flow for dimension corrections at intake; add a verified flag to the boat profile; the router filters candidate vendors by their declared max tow specs before assignment.

risks.risk: Third-party release and ownership disputes: a spouse mid-divorce, a purported buyer, or a lender attempting repossession shows up at the facility for a stored boat.
risks.whyItMatters: Releasing to the wrong party is conversion at full asset value, and a bank's perfected lien on a financed boat can legally outrank the owner's instructions — a judgment call no crew member should ever make at a roll-up door.
risks.mitigation: Hard rule: release only via in-app authorization by the verified account holder naming the recipient, who presents a one-time code plus ID; all repossession or court-order demands route to ops with proof-of-lien documentation before any release; everything logged.
risks.productImplication: The same release_authorization flow as mid-winter access, plus an ops-only exception path for legal demands with document upload and dual sign-off; a one-line vendor training rule surfaced in the crew app: 'never release without the code.'

risks.risk: Fire and hazmat exposure of stored boats: fuel aboard, batteries (increasingly lithium trolling-motor packs), and propane canisters mean one customer's boat can burn the facility and every neighboring boat.
risks.whyItMatters: This is correlated loss plus a subrogation nightmare — if customer A's faulty battery destroys customers B through Z's boats, multiple insurers fight for years and the platform sits in the middle of every conversation. Indoor storage multiplies both probability of ignition sources mattering and the stakes.
risks.mitigation: Intake protocol requires fuel level recorded and stabilized, batteries disconnected or removed (lithium disclosed), and no propane or portable fuel tanks aboard; facility fire-safety items in the vetting checklist; agreement clauses making the owner responsible for undisclosed hazards.
risks.productImplication: Structured intake checklist fields on the condition report (battery_disconnected, fuel_level, stabilizer_added, lithium_aboard, propane_removed); corresponding clauses in the storage agreement; fire-suppression line item in the storage_facilities vetting checklist.

risks.risk: Contents and gear claims: 'my $800 fish finder / tow rope / cooler was in the boat in October and gone in April' — loose personal property that no one can verify either way.
risks.whyItMatters: Contents disputes are the high-frequency, high-emotion, low-dollar category that will dominate support volume; garagekeepers coverage protects the boat, not miscellaneous contents, so there is no insurer to absorb them — only goodwill.
risks.mitigation: Agreement requires valuables removed before haul-out and disclaims liability for loose contents unless inventoried; offer an optional photographed contents inventory as an intake add-on so the honest cases have evidence and the dishonest ones have a deterrent.
risks.productImplication: A contents step in the intake flow: a 'valuables removed' acknowledgment tap plus an optional photo-inventory list stored with the condition report; the disclaimer clause lives in the storage agreement and is quoted automatically in any contents-dispute ticket.
